The battery is a user-changeable off-the-shelf cell, and settings plus tissue loading survive the swap — no service-center visit mid-trip.
An LED warning light backs up the audible alarms, easier to catch in peripheral vision than a beep is to hear through a hood.
Watch-sized on the wrist, and the stock band swaps easily for NATO-style straps.
cons: The buttons sit close together; divers report fumbling into gauge mode while setting nitrox, after which the computer locks out deco calculations for 24 hours.
On-unit history stops at 24 dives, so a busy trip overruns it unless you sync to the app.
The stock watch band draws complaints and is often the first thing replaced.

items.name: Mares Sirius
items.blurb: Mares' flagship watch-style computer, with a color memory-in-pixel display, rechargeable battery and full trimix capability. Built for divers who want one instrument from reef dives to extended-range trips.
items.review: The Sirius is Mares's watch-format flagship, built around a color MIP display that trades brightness for runtime — about 30 hours of diving per charge at medium brightness, 20 with a transmitter connected. It runs Buhlmann ZH-L16C with adjustable gradient factors and predictive multigas across five mixes, plus hoseless integration for up to five transmitters. A tilt-compensated compass with bearing memory and a stopwatch rounds out the tool set, and dives sync over Bluetooth to the Mares app. Charging happens on an included wireless base, so there are no contacts to corrode. Two colorways, Black and Black/Silver, ship on a three-piece quick-change silicone strap, with replacement kits in aqua, blue, red, yellow and black. The display is the honest trade-off: crisp in sunlight, dim on night dives, and one owner reported the backlight refusing to trigger underwater. The strap buckle's spring pin also compresses too easily, letting a knock pop the buckle off — a sore point on a computer near $1,000 before the roughly $370 transmitter.

specs.Battery: Rechargeable, about 30 h diving per charge
specs.Compass: Tilt-compensated digital compass with bearing memory and stopwatch
specs.Logbook: Over 100 hours of dive profile storage on the unit
specs.Strap: Three-piece quick-change silicone strap; optional color kits in aqua, blue, red, yellow and black

pros: About 30 hours of diving per charge at medium screen brightness, roughly 20 with a transmitter connected — the low-power MIP panel is the reason.
Predictive multigas across five mixes with adjustable gradient factors handles staged-deco gas switching.
Tilt-compensated digital compass stores bearings and adds a stopwatch.
Charges on an included wireless base, so there are no charge contacts or port doors to corrode.
Straps swap without tools via a three-piece quick-change kit sold in five colors.
cons: The MIP screen is dim away from sunlight; night divers report straining to read it next to OLED rivals, and one owner had the backlight refuse to trigger underwater.
The strap buckle's spring pin compresses too easily — a light knock can pop the buckle off.
About $1,000 for the computer alone, with the transmitter roughly $370 more.

items.name: Shearwater Perdix 3
items.blurb: Shearwater's full-size flagship, relaunched in May 2026 with an ultra-bright AMOLED screen in a titanium-reinforced case. The default pick for divers heading from recreational into technical territory.
items.review: Shearwater relaunched its flagship in 2026 by swapping the Perdix 2's LCD for a 6.1 cm AMOLED panel at 450 x 600 pixels — four times the resolution — without growing the case, which actually dropped 9 g to 181 g. The bezel is machined titanium and the two buttons are titanium piezo units with no moving parts, so there is nothing mechanical to flood and they respond through thick dry gloves. The spec sheet reads like a technical diver's checklist: 260 m depth rating, Buhlmann ZHL-16C with gradient factors, trimix, fixed-PPO2 CCR/bailout, and air integration for up to 4 Swift transmitters. Power stays deliberately old-fashioned — one user-replaceable AA, good for roughly 60 hours on a lithium cell, with alkaline and rechargeable cells accepted — which matters on a liveaboard a long way from a service centre. The trade-offs are honest ones: US$1,295 buys the computer alone, the full-size housing is conspicuous on a wrist in board shorts, and forum threads point out Garmin offered an AMOLED screen with 8-transmitter support three years earlier. None of that changes the calculus much for divers moving from recreational into technical territory, where the AA battery, the 260 m rating and the glove-proof buttons are the specs that count. It replaces the Perdix 2 as the default full-size pick.

specs.Battery: User-replaceable 1.5 V AA, ~60 h on lithium cell
specs.Modes: OC recreational, OC technical, CC bailout, gauge
specs.Weight: 181 g (9 g lighter than the Perdix 2, same case size)
specs.Controls: Two titanium piezo-electric buttons, no moving parts, usable through thick dry gloves
specs.Connectivity: Bluetooth dive-log download and firmware updates (roughly 6x faster than the Perdix 2)

pros: The 6.1 cm (2.4 in) AMOLED panel runs 450 x 600 pixels — four times the resolution of the Perdix 2's LCD in the same footprint, and readable in direct sunlight
Runs about 60 hours on a single lithium AA you can buy in any airport; alkaline and rechargeable AAs work too, so a flat battery never means a service ticket
260 m depth rating with trimix and fixed-PPO2 CCR modes — one unit covers everything from single-tank reef dives to rebreather work
Titanium piezo buttons have no seals or hinges to flood and register through thick dry gloves
Monitors up to 4 Swift transmitters at once for sidemount, stages or a buddy's tank
cons: US$1,295 before you add a single transmitter
At 181 g with a full-size instrument footprint it wears like a console on the wrist, not a watch — oversized for a warm-water travel kit
Forum divers note the headline features arrived late: Garmin shipped an AMOLED computer tracking 8 transmitters in 2023, and the Perdix 3's answer in 2026 caps at 4

items.name: Shearwater Teric
items.blurb: The watch-style Shearwater that made full technical capability wearable to dinner. Years on, it is still current in the lineup and still the benchmark premium dive watch-computer.
items.review: Years after launch the Teric is still the reference watch-sized dive computer, mostly because Shearwater refused to cut the technical spec to fit the 55 mm case. It runs the same Buhlmann ZHL-16C gradient-factor algorithm as the Perdix line, with trimix, CCR/bailout and gauge modes and a 200 m depth rating, behind a 400 x 400 AMOLED under sapphire crystal with a 316 stainless bezel. At 120 g on a standard 22 mm strap it wears like an ordinary large watch, and it alerts by both vibration and sound — a combination the newer Tern TX dropped. Charging is wireless, on the supplied dock or any Qi pad, and a charge covers 30-plus hours of diving. The weak point is the sealed battery: expected life is about five years, replacement is a service-centre job rather than a screwdriver job, and forum reports of overnight standby drain on some units are common enough to be a pattern, though Shearwater's warranty service draws consistent praise for speed. Air integration with Swift transmitters is supported but each transmitter is a separate purchase on top of the US$1,199 body. Buy it if you want Perdix capability that fits under a shirt cuff; budget for the battery's second life.

specs.Battery: Rechargeable, ~30 h dive mode, wireless charging dock
specs.Modes: OC recreational, OC technical, CC bailout, gauge
specs.Strap: 22 mm standard-fit silicone with stainless buckle; wetsuit extension included

pros: Sapphire crystal over a 400 x 400 AMOLED and a 316 stainless bezel — at 120 g and 55 mm it passes as a daily watch between trips
Both vibration and audible alarms, individually configurable — the cheaper Tern TX ships with vibration only
Full technical mode set including trimix and CCR/bailout to 200 m, unusual in a watch-format computer
Charges on the included dock or any Qi wireless pad, with 30-plus hours of dive time per charge
Standard 22 mm strap fitting takes off-the-shelf watch bands as well as Shearwater's nine colour kits
cons: The battery is factory-sealed with a roughly 5-year life; replacement means shipping the unit to a service centre, and outside the 2-year warranty you pay for the work
ScubaBoard threads document units draining around 10% overnight on standby with the screen off, while identical units lose 2% in two days — behaviour varies unit to unit
US$1,199 before adding a transmitter

items.name: Shearwater Tern TX
items.blurb: Shearwater's most compact computer: a watch-sized recreational unit with air integration at several hundred dollars less than the Teric. The value route into the brand for travelling divers.
items.review: The Tern TX is Shearwater's cheapest route to wireless air integration, and the spec it shares with the flagship is the surprising one: it monitors up to 4 transmitters, matching the Perdix 3, from a 53 mm case weighing 100 g. The 1.3-inch 360 x 360 AMOLED runs the same Buhlmann ZHL-16C gradient-factor algorithm, with air, nitrox, 3-gas nitrox and gauge modes to a 120 m rating. Charging is a wireless USB-C puck rather than a dock, a charge lasts about 20 hours of diving at medium brightness, and standby stretches to six months — divers report covering 20 dives between charges. The 22 mm quick-release straps swap without tools, with silicone bands in five colours and Remora webbing in three. The economies show in three places: alerts are vibration-only where the Teric also sounds an alarm, the lens is toughened alkali-aluminosilicate glass rather than sapphire, and the mode list omits trimix and CCR entirely. That last point defines the buyer — this is a recreational computer with premium hardware, not a technical computer in waiting. At US$775, several hundred less than a Teric, that is a reasonable trade for a travelling single-tank diver.

specs.Battery: Rechargeable, ~20 h at medium brightness, wireless charger included
specs.Strap: 22 mm quick-release; silicone or Remora webbing, tool-free swaps

pros: Reads up to 4 transmitters — the same channel count as the US$1,295 Perdix 3 — from a 100 g watch that costs US$775
1.3-inch 360 x 360 AMOLED in a 53 mm case stays legible in tropical sun at medium brightness
Quick-release 22 mm straps swap without tools, with five silicone and three Remora webbing colours sold
Holds a standby charge for up to 6 months, and owners report a full week of repetitive diving on a single charge
cons: Alerts are vibration-only; Shearwater calls the missing audible alarm a design decision, but Teric owners switching down notice its absence
The lens is toughened glass, not the Teric's sapphire, and it picks up scratches accordingly
Modes stop at 3-gas nitrox and gauge with a 120 m rating — there is no trimix or CCR support, so it is not a computer to grow into technical diving with

items.name: Suunto Nautic
items.blurb: Suunto's late-2025 large-format computer built around a 3.26-inch AMOLED panel — the biggest screen in its class — with GPS route tracking and an integrated LED flashlight.
items.review: The Nautic is Suunto's dedicated big-screen dive computer: a 3.26 in AMOLED panel in a 101 x 60 mm glass-fibre reinforced polyamide case, depth-rated to 200 m and driven by four buttons that work with 8 mm gloves. It runs Bühlmann ZHL-16C with adjustable gradient factors and covers multi-gas, trimix and sidemount with dual tank-pressure readouts from Suunto Tank PODs. Battery life is the headline number — up to 120 hours of diving at medium brightness — and dive-tested reviews report the claim holding across multi-day trips with days off between charges. GPS logs entry and exit points and tracks the route so the Suunto app can replay the dive in 3D, and a small LED flashlight is built into the case. The trade-off is bulk: 232 g with strap, and it is a dive instrument, not something you keep on at dinner. At $699 it costs less than half of Garmin's Descent X50i in the same big-screen class, though divers wanting a longer-proven tech platform still lean Shearwater. For anyone prioritizing legibility and battery life over watch styling, the spec-per-dollar case is strong.

specs.Algorithm: Buhlmann 16 GF (ZHL-16C based) with gradient factors
specs.Air integration: Suunto Tank POD wireless (sold separately), dual-tank sidemount display
specs.Battery: Rechargeable, up to 120 h diving at medium brightness
specs.Extras: Surface GPS with route tracking, integrated LED flashlight
specs.Strap options: Bungee cord, elastic textile, or TPU with stainless steel buckle (kits swap between them)

pros: The 3.26 in AMOLED is the brightest, crispest screen in its price bracket, and the four-button menu works with 8 mm gloves
120 hours of dive time at medium brightness (80 hours at full) — dive-tested reviews confirm multi-day trips on one charge
Bühlmann ZHL-16C with adjustable gradient factors, trimix, GF99/SurfGF readouts and sidemount dual tank-pressure display at $699
GPS marks entry and exit points and tracks the underwater route; the Suunto app replays the dive in 3D
200 m depth rating plus a built-in LED flashlight for reading gauges in dark water
cons: It is big: 232 g and a 101 mm case that sits like a console on smaller wrists — divers coming from watch-sized computers report an adjustment period
At $699 buyers cross-shop the Shearwater Peregrine TX, and the Nautic platform is new; CCR support was promised via a post-launch firmware update rather than shipping in the box

items.name: Garmin Descent X50i
items.blurb: Garmin's first large-format dive computer: a 7.6 cm colour touchscreen with maps, sonar messaging between divers and tank monitoring for up to eight cylinders.
items.review: The Descent X50i is Garmin's first large-format dive computer: a 3 in, 640 x 360 touchscreen in a 62.4 x 98.3 x 24.5 mm fiber-reinforced polymer case with a sapphire lens, rated to 20 ATM (200 m). The feature that separates it from every rival is SubWave sonar — preset messages to buddies up to 30 m away, and live tank pressure, depth and distance for up to eight divers wearing T2 transceivers. Surface GPS, more than 4,000 preloaded dive sites and DiveView maps cover topside navigation, and a built-in LED dive light serves as a backup. Battery runs 13 hours at high brightness, 16 at medium and 20 in night mode — enough for two full days of diving, but early owners reported drain from wake-on-water detection while the unit sat in a bag, only partly cured by firmware. The touchscreen works only at the surface and responds to water drops, so most owners disable it and run the leakproof metal buttons, which handle thick gloves without complaint. At 280 g on the nylon strap and $1,499.99 before transceivers, it is a heavy commitment in both senses. It earns its price with dive teams and instructors who will use the sonar networking; a diver who only needs deco and air integration is paying for capability that stays switched off.

specs.Display: 7.6 cm colour touchscreen with leakproof buttons
specs.Air integration: Descent T2 transceivers via SubWave sonar, up to 8 tanks (sold separately)
specs.Battery: Rechargeable, ~16 h diving at medium brightness (20 h night mode)
specs.Extras: DiveView maps, surface GPS, diver-to-diver messaging, integrated dive light

pros: SubWave sonar sends preset messages to buddies up to 30 m away and shows tank pressure, depth and distance for up to eight divers wearing T2 transceivers
3 in, 640 x 360 touchscreen with leakproof metal buttons that operate fine in thick gloves once the touch layer is switched off
Surface GPS with more than 4,000 preloaded dive sites and DiveView maps, plus automatic entry/exit point marking
Built-in LED dive light doubles as a backup if a primary light floods or fails
20 ATM (200 m) rating and sapphire lens on a computer that also runs Garmin's full sports-watch feature set with 32 GB of storage
cons: Battery is 13-20 hours per charge depending on brightness, and owners reported units draining in the dive bag from wake-on-water detection even when powered off — firmware updates improved but did not equal the Descent Mk series
The touchscreen works only at the surface and reacts to water drops, so many owners keep it disabled; some also report inconsistent power-button behavior
280 g on the nylon strap and $1,499.99 before T2 transceivers, and the glass runs larger than the active display, leaving a black border

items.name: Scubapro Galileo 3 (G3)
items.blurb: Watch-style successor to the G2 with a full-color display under sapphire glass, a stainless steel case and a rechargeable battery. A recent line-wide price cut makes it one of the better values in full-feature computers.
items.review: The Galileo 3 compresses Scubapro's full Galileo feature set into a 47.2 mm watch-style case: stainless steel housing, sapphire lens and a rotating bezel coated in self-luminescent material, rated to 120 m in dive mode and 300 m in gauge. You choose between two algorithms — predictive multi-gas ZHL-16 ADT MB, which feeds tank pressure and workload from the optional transmitter into the deco calculation, or ZHL-16 GF for divers who prefer to set gradient factors themselves. Scuba mode covers trimix, CCR, sidemount and PMG, and the full-text four-button interface carries over from the G2, so there is no icon deciphering underwater. The rechargeable lithium cell delivers about 30 hours of diving per charge and refills through a proprietary USB dongle in roughly 80 minutes; there is no contactless charging. The screen is the weak point — owners report a low-resolution layout with pixelated fonts that reads like a shrunk console display rather than a design built for a watch face. Weight is the other compromise, enough that Scubapro sells a titanium version at 99 g, nearly 40% lighter, at a higher price. Street prices have dropped well below launch, which makes the standard G3 a better value now than when it shipped.

specs.Display: Full-color LCD under scratch-resistant sapphire glass
specs.Algorithms: ZHL-16 ADT MB PMG (predictive multi-gas) or ZHL-16 GF, selectable
specs.Connectivity: Bluetooth LE log download (iOS, Android, PC/Mac)
specs.Modes: Scuba, Gauge, Apnea; Scuba adds Trimix, CCR, Sidemount and PMG settings

pros: Two selectable algorithms: predictive multi-gas ZHL-16 ADT MB that folds tank pressure and workload into the deco calculation, or plain ZHL-16 GF for divers who set their own gradient factors
Air integration reports true remaining bottom time, not just pressure, and supports multiple transmitters for PMG and sidemount
Stainless housing, sapphire lens and a self-luminescent rotating bezel, rated to 120 m in dive mode and 300 m in gauge
Full-text menus on four buttons carry over from the G2 console, so nothing needs an icon glossary
About 30 hours of diving per charge, with Bluetooth Low Energy sync to iOS, Android, PC and Mac
cons: The display resolution is low for the class — owners describe pixelated fonts and a layout that looks like a console screen shrunk to watch size
Charging needs a proprietary USB dongle and takes about 80 minutes from the low-battery warning; there is no drop-on charging as on some rivals
The steel case is heavy for daily wear — Scubapro's own titanium version cuts weight to 99 g, nearly 40% less, but costs more

categories.title: Top Dive Computers in 2026
categories.article_intro: No category of dive gear has moved faster. Ten years ago a colour screen was exotic; today the mid-market standard is a phone-bright AMOLED display, Bluetooth log sync and a rechargeable battery, and the watch-style computers double as daily fitness trackers. Two brands anchor the top of every reviewer's list: Shearwater, whose Peregrine and Perdix 2 are the recreational and tec benchmarks respectively, and Garmin, whose Descent range won over divers who want one watch for everything. Below them, the Zoop Novo, Leonardo and Puck Pro+ keep doing what they've always done — reliable, readable decompression guidance for the price of a fin set.
tips: Buy the screen you can read at 30m with fogged lenses — big numerals beat extra features.
Conservative vs liberal algorithms differ by minutes of no-deco time; Bühlmann with adjustable gradient factors is the transparent choice.
Air integration is genuinely useful but adds a transmitter (~$300) — budget for it or skip it consciously.
If it's also your watch, check battery life in dive mode; if it's dive-only, a user-changeable battery saves service costs.

categories.title: Top Wetsuits in 2026
categories.article_intro: Neoprene thickness tracks water temperature, but the ranges overlap more than any chart admits — how cold you run, how long your dives last and whether you add a hood all move the line. As a working guide: 2 mm and shorties above about 27°C, 3 mm from roughly 24 to 29°C, 5 mm through the mid-teens to low twenties, and 7 mm once you're in the low teens or below. Pick a thickness for the suits and prices at that rating.

thickness_groups.label: 2 mm / shorty — warm tropical
thickness_groups.article_intro: Above 27°C most divers cook inside a 5 mm before they even leave the surface. A 2 mm full suit or a shorty holds off the slow chill of a 60-minute dive and keeps hydroids, fire coral and the sun off your skin without the bulk. A shorty drops the arm and leg coverage for less material and a lower price; a thin full suit costs a little warmth-per-gram but covers everything.
tips: A shorty covers the core and leaves arms and legs free — cooler and cheaper; a thin full suit adds sting and sun protection on longer or deeper dives.
Flatlock seams are fine at this thickness: they lie flat and comfortable, and a little water exchange is no problem in warm water.
Look for a back-zip with a long leash and smooth-skin neck for easy solo entry between dives.

items.name: O'Neill Reactor-2 2mm Back Zip Springsuit
items.blurb: A stretchy, bombproof warm-water shorty that shrugs off boat decks and tank abrasion.
items.review: The Reactor-2 is O'Neill's entry-level spring suit, and the diving case for it is simple: surf-brand pricing with a few features cheap dive shorties skip. The body and legs use FluidFlex neoprene while the sleeves get stretchier UltraFlex panels, so arm swing on long surface swims does not fight the suit. The Smoothskin Fluid Foam collar seals better than the stitched fabric collars on most flatlock shorties, and Krypto Knee Padz handle kneeling on a dive deck. Seams are flatlock throughout, which means slow constant water exchange — owners report comfort at 18-21°C with a floor around 16°C, so for scuba treat it as a 24°C-plus tropical suit with margin. Fit runs slim: one buyer sized up to XL per the chart and still fought the shoulders and zip, though tall cuts widen the range — an LT fit a 185 cm, 88 kg diver correctly. The recurring complaint is the back zipper, with several reports of failures after minimal use. As a warm-water travel shorty it undercuts dive-brand equivalents on price without reading as a compromise.

specs.Neck closure: Smoothskin Fluid Foam collar with adjustable single seal
specs.Sleeve panels: UltraFlex stretch neoprene (higher-stretch than the body panels)
specs.Sizes: S-3XL plus tall cuts (LT confirmed at retail)

pros: UltraFlex sleeve panels stretch more than the FluidFlex body, so shoulder rotation on surface swims costs less effort than the price suggests
Krypto Knee Padz cover kneeling on dive decks — most spring suits at this price leave the knees as plain neoprene
The Smoothskin Fluid Foam collar seals against flushing better than the stitched fabric collars on typical flatlock shorties
Tall sizes exist: an LT fit a 185 cm, 88 kg buyer correctly, which few budget shorties accommodate
cons: Runs slim — one buyer who sized up to XL per the chart still found it very tight across the shoulders and hard to zip
Several owner reviews report back-zipper failures after light use
Flatlock seams exchange water constantly, so it weeps below roughly 24°C

items.name: ScubaPro Sport Shorty 2mm
items.blurb: Buttery-soft 2mm neoprene cut specifically for the shoulder mobility divers need.
items.review: ScubaPro's Sport Shorty is the plainest suit in its catalog and reads like it: 2 mm neoprene, polyester lining inside and out, flatlock seams, back zip. X-Foam is the interesting part — a limestone-based, petroleum-free neoprene certified against PAH and REACH chemical limits, documentation most entry-level shorties do not carry. The triathlon cut borrowed from the Sport steamers keeps shoulders and armpits free, which matters most in a shorty used for surface swims and snorkeling between dives. The YKK back zip runs a brass slider rather than plastic, a small spec that decides how the suit looks after two seasons in salt. Flatlock construction means steady water exchange, so treat it as a 26°C-plus suit rather than pushing its lower edge. Sizing is the real catch: ScubaPro cuts slim and European, and forum advice is to go up at least one size from US habits — the 2XS-4XL men's run leaves room to do it. Expect to pay $20-30 over comparable Cressi or Bare shorties for the brand and the chemistry.

specs.Neoprene base: X-Foam, limestone-based and petroleum-free; complies with PAH and REACH chemical limits
specs.Cut: Triathlon cut carried over from the Sport steamers

pros: X-Foam limestone neoprene carries PAH and REACH compliance documentation, which almost no entry-level shorty offers
The triathlon cut leaves shoulders and armpits unrestricted for surface swims and snorkel intervals between dives
The brass YKK zip slider resists salt corrosion better than the plastic sliders on cheaper shorties
Men's sizes run 2XS-4XL, a wider spread than most 2 mm shorties
cons: ScubaPro cuts to slim European proportions — forum consensus is to go up at least one size from US sizing habits
At roughly $90-100 it costs $20-30 more than comparable flatlock shorties from Cressi or Bare
Flatlock seams exchange water steadily, so it is a 26°C-plus suit rather than a true 2 mm-rating stretch

items.name: Cressi Lido 2mm Shorty (Women's)
items.blurb: An anatomically cut women's 2mm shorty that's light, flattering and easy to travel with.
items.review: The Lido is Cressi's cheapest way into neoprene and behaves accordingly: 2 mm double-lined foam, flat stitching in scratch-resistant thread, and a front YKK plastic zip that makes it fully self-donning. That zip is the practical argument — nothing to reach behind your back for on a rocking boat, and the pull sits low enough on the neck not to pinch. Double lining on both faces handles abrasion better than the single-lined suits common at this price, and the sleeve and leg openings are hemmed in neoprene rather than left raw. In the water it does what any 2 mm shorty can: sting and sun protection plus modest insulation for 26°C and warmer, with owners reporting no cold spots through the lower back when the fit is right. Fit is the catch — it runs one to two sizes small, and a 170 cm, 73 kg buyer who pre-emptively ordered XL over her usual L still found it very tight. Torso length draws opposite complaints from tall and petite divers, and larger-busted divers report the zipper working its way down. It is a competent boat-bag shorty at a low price, not a fitted dive suit.

specs.Edge finish: Sleeve and leg openings hemmed with neoprene

pros: The front YKK plastic zip makes it fully self-donning — no fishing for a back-zip leash on a moving boat, and the pull sits low enough not to press at the throat
Double-lined neoprene on both faces takes abrasion better than the single-lined suits common at this price
Sleeve and leg openings are hemmed in neoprene rather than raw-cut, so the edges resist fraying
One of the cheapest name-brand 2 mm shorties sold through dive retailers
cons: Runs one to two sizes small — a 170 cm, 73 kg buyer who ordered XL over her usual L still found it very tight
Torso length draws opposite complaints from tall and petite divers, so try-on matters more than the chart
Larger-busted divers report the front zipper creeping down during use

items.name: Bare 2mm Sport S-Flex Shorty
items.blurb: Feather-light stretch neoprene at a price that makes it the easy warm-water carry-on.
items.review: Bare built the Sport S-Flex shorty around one idea: full-stretch neoprene everywhere, so an entry-level suit stops feeling like a wrestling match. The S-Flex blend plus a 53 cm heavy-duty back zipper gets the suit on and off faster than most 2 mm shorties, and seamless underarm panels delete the flatlock seam exactly where shorties chafe during surface swims. The 2 mm Glideskin collar closes with Velcro and seals the neck without a stitched edge digging in. Construction is flatseam throughout, so it exchanges water continuously — treat it as a 26°C-plus suit, like every flatlock shorty. Articulated arm and leg patterning gives it a pre-bent shape that reduces bunching behind the knees and elbows. Bare backs it with a 2-year materials and workmanship warranty, average for the class. The caveat in 2026 is availability: Bare's own site files it under outlet/clearance, so size runs are patchy and a restock in your size is not guaranteed — if Black or Blue is still on a shelf in your size, it is one of the easier recommendations in budget warm-water neoprene.
pros: S-Flex full-stretch neoprene plus the 53 cm back-zip opening makes this one of the fastest 2 mm shorties to don and doff
Seamless underarm panels remove the flatlock seam exactly where shorties chafe on long surface swims
The 2 mm Glideskin collar with Velcro adjustment seals the neck without a stitched edge digging in
Street prices start around $80, at the low end for a dive-brand shorty
cons: Bare's own site now lists it under outlet/clearance, so size runs are patchy and restocks are not guaranteed
Back-zip entry needs the pull leash or a buddy on a pitching deck

items.name: Henderson Thermoprene Pro 3mm Front Zip Shorty
items.blurb: A front-zip 3mm shorty with 250% stretch neoprene that donns almost like a jacket.
items.review: The Thermoprene Pro shorty is built like Henderson's cold-water suits shrunk to 3 mm: every seam is glued and blind-stitched instead of flatlock, so it does not weep the way nearly every other shorty in this class does. The neoprene stretches 250% against 180% for the original Thermoprene, and the Aqua Silk lining on all interior panels slides over dry or damp skin, so the front #10 YKK zip with its stainless slider is almost redundant for donning — but it makes the suit fully self-service on a crowded deck. Owners also note it holds its shape when wet rather than going baggy in spots like the older Thermoprene. The men's size run goes XS to 6XL, the widest spread here, with patterns drafted and fit-tested in the USA. Henderson backs it with a 10-year limited warranty that covers the zipper, against the 1-2 years standard elsewhere. The costs are real: street price lands around double a flatlock shorty, and frequent divers report that high-stretch foam like this compresses and loses insulation faster than stiffer neoprene under heavy use. Fit runs snug, so between sizes go up. For a week of repetitive 26-29°C boat dives the sealed seams justify the premium; for occasional snorkel trips they do not.

specs.Pattern origin: Designed, patterned and fit-tested in the USA

250% stretch neoprene against 180% in the standard Thermoprene, and it holds its shape wet instead of going baggy
10-year limited warranty including the zipper, against the 1-2 years standard for warm-water suits

The front #10 YKK zip with stainless slider makes it fully self-donning

items.name: Aqualung HydroFlex 3mm Shorty
items.blurb: Eco-minded, PAH-free stretch neoprene shorty tuned for warm-water scuba comfort.
items.review: Aqualung's warm-water shorty is cut from 3 mm limestone-based neoprene with a PAH-free formula that the company rates at up to 250% more stretch than standard foam, and it shows in the donning: the suit goes over dry skin without a fight. Flatlock seams sit flat against bare skin, and O-ring seals at the arm and thigh openings reduce the flushing most shorties accept as a given. Closure is a heavy #10 YKK back zip with a pull-tab leash and a shield behind the teeth; a skin-out chest panel blocks wind chill on the boat between dives. The weak point is fit rather than build: the pattern runs slim through the chest and neck, and owners on ScubaBoard report ending up outside the size chart or having the collar tailored. US retail runs from about $119 for older colorways to $199 for current ones, which keeps it in impulse-buy territory next to a full-length 3 mm. For tropical water over a swimsuit, it covers the core and skips the bulk, which is the whole argument for a shorty.

specs.Seals: O-ring seals at arm and thigh openings
specs.Zipper hardware: #10 YKK back zip with pull-tab leash and zipper shield

items.name: ScubaPro Everflex Steamer 3/2mm
items.blurb: A thin full-length steamer that punches above its millimeters for full-body coverage.
items.review: The Everflex Steamer puts 3 mm X-Foam neoprene over the torso and 2 mm on the limbs, and the combination carries a CE Class C rating for 16-24°C water — a classification very few 3 mm suits achieve. Construction backs the rating: panels joined with solvent-free water-based Aqua glue, glued and blind-stitched outer seams, and a Diamond Span inner lining that drains water and adds stretch for donning. Cuffs are fuse-cut rather than sealed, so the suit pulls on quickly, and Tatex pads protect knees and shoulders from tank and reef contact. The trap is sizing: ScubaBoard threads agree the cut runs one to two sizes small, and divers with broad shoulders or thick arms should try before buying. The diagonal rear YKK zipper with its brass slider is durable but unreachable solo, and some backplate divers find it lands in the wrong place. Men's suits come in black only; the women's cut adds a white colorway and runs from 2XS to 4XL with short and tall variants. With ScubaPro pushing the $459 Yulex replacement, the classic X-Foam steamer is increasingly a discounted find rather than a stocked staple.

items.name: Cressi Lido 2mm Full Wetsuit
items.blurb: Full arm-and-leg 2mm coverage that's still cool enough for the warmest reefs.
items.review: The Lido is Cressi's entry 2 mm steamer, and its defining feature is the YKK plastic front zip that runs below the navel: the suit goes on like a jacket, needs no buddy, and the zipper cannot corrode. The 2 mm neoprene is soft enough to conform to most builds, and the pattern deletes seams where they hurt — the underarms are seamless and the armpits and crotch get anatomical panels, so nothing digs during long surface swims. Torso seams are double-stitched; the wrist and ankle hems are single-stitched, which is where the $99.95 price shows. In-water testing found no leaks or cold spots at the lower back, provided the fit is right. Fit is the catch: the Lido runs small, with buyers regularly going up two or three sizes, and the torso length splits opinion between tall and petite divers. Cressi backs it with a 2-year limited warranty, unusual at this price. As a tropical suit for 26°C-plus water or a layer under a thicker suit, it does the job for roughly half the cost of the brand-name alternatives.

items.name: Waterproof W30 2.5mm Women's
items.blurb: A light, anatomically sculpted 2.5mm full suit for warm-water trips where a shorty is not quite enough. The women's cut follows a genuinely different pattern, not a shrunk men's suit.
items.review: Waterproof's W30 takes the opposite approach to the super-stretch crowd: standard-modulus 2.5 mm CR neoprene, cut precisely instead of stretched into shape. The 3D sculpted pattern comes with pre-bent arms and legs and a gender-specific women's cut, and the fit reviews are consistent — divers describe it going on and off easily dry and fitting closely through the torso, with one owner finding it warmer in the water than her old 3 mm from another brand. Details are dive-specific rather than surf-derived: PU-embossed panels on the shoulders and seat grip a BCD and shrug off tank-strap wear, polyurethane knee pads take the contact, and the WPAD dock on the right hip accepts a zip-on expandable pocket sold separately. The extra-long YKK back zipper uses a stainless-steel slider. The design trades away sealing: neck, wrists and ankles are finished with rolled-neoprene piping rather than seals, so the W30 flushes when pushed into cooler water than it was drawn for. At $199 at Scuba.com in women's XS through XL, it costs more than commodity 2.5 mm suits and justifies it on pattern-making rather than materials.

Some owners report the wrist openings run loose relative to the otherwise snug cut

thickness_groups.label: 3 mm — tropical / warm water
thickness_groups.article_intro: 3 mm is the default for most tropical diving — the Caribbean, the Red Sea in summer, the reefs of the Coral Triangle. It carries enough heat for three or four dives a day in 25–28°C water without leaving you sweating on the boat in between, and a good one rolls small enough to pack beside your regulator. Drop below about 24°C, though, and most divers start reaching for a 5 mm or a hood.
tips: Glued-and-blindstitched seams keep water out far better than flatlock, and matter more as your dives get longer.
A snug fit is what keeps you warm — a 3 mm that fits well beats a 5 mm that gaps at the lower back and floods.
Fabric-lined interiors slide on easily when you're damp; extra chest and back panels add real warmth for the weight.

items.name: Fourth Element Proteus II 3mm
items.blurb: The benchmark tropical dive suit: warmer, stretchier and tougher than almost anything else at 3mm.
items.review: The Proteus II is built on a simple premise: a 3 mm suit leaks heat mostly through water exchange, so Fourth Element sealed the exchange paths instead of adding rubber. The Hydrolock system runs an internal neoprene section from the small of the back over the head, forming a barrier between the rear zipper and the skin, while Glideskin wrist seals and internal polyurethane ankle seals close the limbs; seams are double-glued, blind-stitched and taped. The result, confirmed across published tests and owner threads, is a 3 mm that behaves like a conventional 5 mm — testers report flushing water in deliberately to cool off in the 23°C-plus range the maker recommends. The price of that warmth is the donning ritual: the neck seal arrives tight enough that owners describe being choked until they stretch it over a container for a couple of days, and the internal zip dam slides down during donning with no way to reach it, so a buddy is close to mandatory. Fit runs snug overall because the seals only work when they touch skin, and sizing up is the standard advice despite a 16-cut men's range from XS to XXXL with short and tall variants. At $692 on Fourth Element's US store, it is priced against 5 mm flagships rather than other 3 mm suits, and it only comes in black with white shoulder trim. It is the suit for divers who chill easily but refuse the buoyancy and bulk penalty of thicker neoprene.

specs.Zip: Back zip with anti-flush spine flap
specs.Neck seal: Hydrolock over-the-head inner neck seal with internal zip barrier
specs.Sizes: Men's XS-XXXL in 16 cuts including short and tall variants

At $692 on Fourth Element's US store, it costs more than many 5 mm suits; the snug seal-driven fit also pushes buyers a size up

items.name: Scubapro Definition Steamer 3mm
items.blurb: One of the few suits engineered purely for divers, with an infrared fleece lining that punches above its 3mm rating.
items.review: The Definition is built on Scubapro's Body Map System, a 3D-cut pattern that puts Ultraspan across the chest and Stretchtec panels down the sides, so the stretch sits where a diver actually bends. The foam is X-Foam, a limestone-based, petroleum-free neoprene with nylon on both faces, and an orange infrared lining covers the front and back torso to reflect body heat back at the diver. It carries a CE Class D rating for water of 22°C and warmer, which is exactly its brief. Detailing is more thorough than most 3mm suits get: printed abrasion panels on shoulders, elbows and knees, and a diagonal rear YKK zipper with a brass slider that is easier to reach solo than a vertical zip. The catch is the cut — it follows a slim European block, and divers regularly report needing a size or two above the chart, so try it on rather than trusting the numbers. Men get a single yellow-on-black colorway. At about US$340 it costs more than a Henderson Thermoprene Pro and less than a Bare Reactive, with the most exterior reinforcement of the three.

specs.Reinforcements: Abrasion-resistant printed shoulder, elbow and knee panels

pros: Infrared-reflective lining across the front and back torso adds warmth without adding millimeters
Printed abrasion panels on shoulders, elbows and knees cover the wear points a 3mm suit usually loses first
Diagonal rear YKK zipper with a brass slider is easier to reach solo than a straight vertical back zip

items.name: Bare Reactive 3mm
items.blurb: A stitch-free, graphene-lined suit that turns your own body heat into extra warmth with almost no seam leakage.
items.review: The Reactive is Bare's warmest 3mm and priced like it — listings at US retailers now run around US$550. The lining is Graphene OMNIRED, woven with 13 thermo-reactive minerals that return body heat as infrared; Bare's lab testing claims an average 5°C gain over its previous OMNIRED fabric. Construction matches the lining: the No Stitch Technology process leaves the interior free of stitching, seams are taped inside and out, and the underarms are seamless. The 53 cm S-Lock2 back zipper has offset teeth and PU-coated tape to slow water entry, and Armor Flex 2.0 moulded kneepads handle deck wear. The size run is the deepest in this group, S to 4XL plus eight short and tall cuts, though the cut itself is snug and divers commonly advise sizing up from the chart. The recurring complaint is the zipper: forum reports include failures inside 20-25 dives, once on both an original suit and its warranty replacement, and Bare reps say the part has since been revised. For divers who run cold in 24-26°C water it is the warmest 3mm here; everyone else is paying for insulation they may not need.

specs.Sizes: Men's S-4XL plus short and tall cuts (MLS, LS, XLS, 2XLS, MT, MLT, LT, XLT)

items.name: Henderson Thermoprene Pro 3mm
items.blurb: A front-zip workhorse with 250% stretch that pulls on easily and seals out flushing.
items.review: Thermoprene Pro is Henderson's answer to divers who found the original Thermoprene stiff: the material stretches 250% against the old fabric's 180%, and it goes on and comes off without the wrestling a 3mm usually demands. Henderson also refuses to flatlock — even this 3mm gets glued, blind-stitched seams, a construction most brands reserve for 5mm and up, so less water migrates through the stitching. Hardware is a #10 YKK zipper with a stainless slider, DuraTex stretch knee pads, and an Aqua Silk interior that slides over damp skin. Warranty terms are unusually long for a wetsuit: 10 years on the zipper, 5 on materials. The size run covers XS-3XL with short and tall cuts, and the suit is sold in separate back-zip and front-zip versions rather than one compromise entry. Divers who have owned each generation report the Pro holds its shape when wet instead of going baggy. The trade-off is price — expect US$50-100 over the standard Thermoprene — and men's colors stop at black and black/blue.

items.name: Cressi Morea 3mm
items.blurb: The honest value pick for pure warm-water diving: solid warmth and stretch without the premium markup.
items.review: The Morea is the budget entry here, usually US$143-180, and Cressi spends the money where it matters. The body is 3mm neoprene with Ultraspan super-stretch panels on the lower arms and legs, the zones where donning effort concentrates. A rubberised smooth-skin chest panel reduces drag, the knees get anti-abrasion patches, and the rear YKK zipper carries an Aquastop flap plus a hook-and-loop collar with a second tab to keep the pull flat. It is rated for water of 22°C and warmer, the same bracket as suits costing twice as much. The compromises are predictable: the interior is plain nylon with no reflective or fleece lining, so it gives up warmth to lined suits of the same thickness on repetitive days. Fit reports are consistent — the torso runs a little stiff, the legs are cut short, and most reviewers recommend buying one size above the chart. The same pattern is sold in man, lady and junior cuts.

specs.Collar: Hook-and-loop closure with second tab to hold the zip pull flat

items.name: Pinnacle Tempo XT 3mm Merino
items.blurb: A merino-lined 3mm that dives noticeably warmer than its thickness and costs less than its rivals.
items.review: The Tempo XT lines its chest and back with actual merino wool, a material choice no other suit in this class makes, and Pinnacle claims up to 35% more insulating power from the panels. Divers back that up in practice, reporting Red Sea trips in this 3mm where a 5mm would be the norm, and the wool stays comfortable when the suit goes on wet. The neoprene is titanium-lined Elastiprene, stretchier than earlier Pinnacle fabric, with double-glued, blind-stitched seams and stress disks at internal seam intersections. Convenience details are unusually complete: 20 cm ankle zippers for booted feet, a smooth-skin-sealed spine pad with a guard that stops the zipper biting your back, printed rubber shoulder pads to hold a BCD, and multi-panel rubber knees. It comes in 13 men's sizes but one color, black. The lanolin in merino resists odor and the lining dries fast, though some Amazon reviewers report a smell settling in when the suit isn't rinsed and dried properly. The bigger risk is availability: scuba.com lists it at closeout pricing, about US$247 against a US$329.95 list, and tall sizes come and go.

specs.Zip: Back zip with 10mm spine flap, ankle zips

items.name: Aqua Lung Aquaflex 3mm
items.blurb: Four-way stretch neoprene with liquid-sealed seams for a comfortable, leak-resistant tropical fit.
items.review: The Aquaflex is Aqua Lung's stretch-first suit in its warm-water thickness, and the stretch is the whole argument: 4-way neoprene claimed at three times the give of standard material, with pre-bent arms and legs that stop the fabric bunching behind knees and elbows. Donning is where owners notice it — reviewers report an accurate fit straight out of the box, helped by a men's size run of 13 fits including long and short variants like MLL, LS and XLS. The seams are sealed with liquid rubber rather than left as bare stitching, which matters more in a thin suit where any flush is felt immediately. The back zip uses a 3-way seal backed by a spine pad, so the usual cold line down the spine is largely absent and the tank sits against padding instead of vertebrae. Wrists and ankles get spandex-taped edges that seal well but want gentle handling. The trade-off is the one that always comes with soft foam: at $369 you are paying stretch-suit money, and the same suppleness that makes it easy to don means it will not shrug off boat ladders and Velcro the way a stiffer suit does. For divers who find standard 3 mm suits a wrestling match, that trade is usually worth making.

The 3-way back-zip seal with spine pad cuts flush-through along the zipper and pads the tank against your back
cons: $369 list is at the top of the 3 mm price band

items.name: Bare Evoke 3mm Women's
items.blurb: A women's-specific suit shaped by an all-female design team, with infrared lining for extra warmth.
items.review: The Evoke is Bare's warmth play for women, and the mechanism is unusually specific: a graphene carbon-nanostructure lining layered over Omnired fabric, whose 13 thermo-reactive minerals convert body heat into infrared and bounce it back at the skin. Bare's own lab testing puts the graphene version 5°C warmer on average than the previous Omnired-only lining, which in practice means staying comfortable through a third dive that would end early in a plain 3 mm suit. Construction backs the lining up — seamless underarms, skin-to-skin internal zipper flaps, and a hook-and-loop 360-degree collar that trims water exchange at the neck. The 53 cm (21 in) G-Lock 2 back zipper with offset teeth is genuinely easy to run up single-handed. The pattern comes from Bare's all-female design team and fits most divers well, though owner reports disagree: baggy shoulders and thighs for some, a long torso for shorter divers, so trying one on beats ordering blind. The other caveats are price and longevity — $554.95 is serious money, and a minority of owners report cuff and lining wear inside a year. When the fit works, it is about the warmest 3 mm a woman can buy; the premium is for the lining, not the durability.

items.name: Beuchat Optima 2
items.blurb: Current second generation of Beuchat's warm-water workhorse, a 3 mm men's one-piece with a preformed anatomical cut and dope-dyed neoprene that cuts dye-process water use by 35 to 40 percent.
items.review: The Optima 2 is Beuchat's mid-range warm-and-temperate suit, sold in 3 mm and 5 mm without hood alongside a hooded 7 mm. Beuchat splits the neoprene by function: Elaskin X.6.2 stretch through the body, with the noticeably softer X.6.4 under the arms so reaching for a valve or clipping a stage does not load the shoulders. The collar is zipperless with a glottis cutout, which sounds like catalogue language but simply means one fewer place for water to pump in at the neck. Wrists and ankles get smooth-skin seals — ankle zips and an Easy Wave wrist system handle donning — and the pre-shaped anatomical cut is designed to eliminate the water pockets that make a loose suit cold. Wear points are covered with Supratex 2 knees and PU prints on the seat and shoulders. The foam is limestone- and recycled-rubber-based, dyed with a Dope Dyed process Beuchat says cuts water use by 35-40%. Owner feedback in French dive shops centres on value: easy on, easy off, priced under €200 even in the hooded 7 mm. The catch for many readers is simply getting one — the dealer network is overwhelmingly European.

items.name: Waterproof W5 3.5mm
items.blurb: Waterproof's tropical entry in its handmade Pro Series, cut from the same premium limestone neoprene as the W7. A 3.5mm for divers who want cold-water build quality in warm seas.
items.review: The W5 is Waterproof's tropical suit built to cold-water standards, and the 3.5 mm thickness is the first tell — half a millimetre more than the category norm, in 100% Microcell CR limestone neoprene that resists compression instead of pancaking at 30 m. Construction is glued and blindstitched by hand, with Duratex panels on the seat and elbows and wave-flex zones at the arm and leg joints. Hardware is where the price shows: a YKK No. 10 Vislon back zipper with stainless slider, YKK zips at wrists and ankles, and two stretch hip pockets with drain valves that hold a spool and DSMB. The size chart is the practical argument — 16 men's and 12 women's sizes including tall and plus cuts, which is why travelling divers who never fit off-the-rack suits end up here, though several owners report the chart itself runs small and sized up. The complaints are real: US street prices around $450-500 are double the category, and more than one ScubaBoard owner has had glued cuff seams open or seam threads pull loose, which should not happen at this price. Buy it for the fit range and the compression resistance; budget shoppers have cheaper ways to stay warm at 26°C.

items.name: Pinnacle Cruiser 3mm
items.blurb: A budget 3mm that borrows features from suits costing twice as much: titanium lining, smooth-skin seals and ankle zips. One of the best value warm-water full suits at dive retailers.
items.review: The Cruiser is Pinnacle's entry suit, and its pitch is construction that normally costs more: titanium-lined nylon-2 neoprene through the whole suit, seams that are double glued and blind stitched with stress disks at every intersection, and a 5 mm spine pad filling the gap along the back zipper. That pad does two jobs — it blocks the internal water channel that chills most budget back-zip suits and cushions the tank. The rolled neck closes with hook-and-loop rather than a raw edge, and higher-stretch neoprene in the extremities keeps donning manageable despite the warm, denser body panels. Wear points get roughskin kneepads and printed rubber shoulder pads that also stop a BCD wandering on a long surface swim. At $229.95 street it undercuts stretch-flagship 3 mm suits by $140 or more while sealing its seams better than most of them. The honest drawbacks are supply and cosmetics: Pinnacle's distribution has been erratic for years, with forum reports of backorders and shops that stopped carrying the brand, and the exposed stitching tends to fray at the surface even though the glued seams underneath hold. As a warm-water workhorse bought on function per dollar, it is hard to argue with.

specs.Neoprene: Titanium-lined, high-stretch arm and leg panels
specs.Seams: Glued and sewn with smooth-skin seals
specs.Spine pad: 5 mm pad along the back zipper
specs.Shoulder pads: Printed rubber panels that grip BCD straps

pros: Titanium-lined nylon-2 neoprene throughout reflects body heat, a lining usually reserved for suits well above this price
The 5 mm spine pad fills the channel along the back zipper, cutting the internal water movement that makes cheap back-zip suits cold
Seams are double glued and blind stitched with reinforcing stress disks at intersections — construction, not the flatlock stitching typical at this price
Roughskin kneepads and printed rubber shoulder panels keep the BCD from sliding and take the wear a rental-grade suit can't
cons: Pinnacle's dealer network is thin; ScubaBoard threads describe long backorders and shops dropping the brand, so finding your size in stock can take patience
Surface stitching frays with use — cosmetic, since the seams are glued and blind stitched both sides, but it looks worn early

thickness_groups.label: 5 mm — temperate / subtropical
thickness_groups.article_intro: 5 mm covers the awkward middle — Mediterranean summers, Pacific thermoclines, the subtropical diving that's too cold for 3 mm and doesn't yet justify 7 mm. The extra couple of millimetres roughly doubles how long you last before the cold registers, and a hood and vest stretch a well-fitted 5 mm down toward 14–15°C. Fit counts for more here than at any other thickness: a suit that gaps at the back flushes cold water with every kick.
tips: Seals make the suit: smooth-skin or glide-skin wrist and ankle cuffs cut the cold-water flush that steals your warmth.
High-stretch neoprene is worth paying for at 5 mm — thicker rubber fights you unless it's engineered to flex.
Add a matching hood and vest for another few degrees; most of your heat is lost through your head and core.

items.name: Bare Reactive 5mm
items.blurb: Graphene-lined full-stretch warmth that reflects your own body heat back at you.
items.review: The Reactive is BARE's warmest 5mm and the price reflects it, at around $600 US. Its case rests on two mechanisms rather than thicker rubber: a Graphene Omnired torso lining with 13 thermoreactive minerals that converts body heat to infrared and reflects it back (BARE's lab work shows an average 5°C gain over the previous Omnired fabric), and No Stitch Technology seams that are glued and taped inside and out with no needle perforations to weep. In the water that adds up to owners comparing it to other brands' 7mm suits, and the seam-free interior keeps the second dive of the day less miserable. The Elastek neoprene stretches enough that a wet suit goes back on without a fight, and the 20-size run from roughly 45 to 136 kg includes short and tall cuts. The weak point in owner reports is the back zipper, with multiple accounts of failures inside 25 dives; the 2-year warranty covers it, but a zipper dying mid-trip is a real cost. The infrared claims invite skepticism, and you cannot isolate them from the fit, but the warmth itself is consistently reported. Buy it for cold-for-the-thickness diving in the 16–24°C band; buy something cheaper if you dive twice a year.

specs.Sizes: 20 adult sizes covering roughly 45–136 kg, including short and tall cuts
specs.Claimed thermal gain: average +5°C over BARE's previous Omnired lining in lab tests
specs.Infrared lining minerals: 13 thermoreactive minerals woven into the Graphene Omnired torso fabric

pros: The Graphene Omnired torso lining reflects body heat back as infrared; BARE measured an average 5°C gain over its previous Omnired fabric, and owners regularly report the suit diving as warm as other brands' 7mm.
No Stitch Technology seams are glued and taped inside and out with zero needle holes, so there is no seam seepage and the interior stays noticeably drier over a two-tank day.
Elastek full-stretch neoprene makes a wet second donning realistic without a struggle, unusual for a suit this warm.
The 20-size run (short, regular and tall cuts, roughly 45–136 kg) means most divers get a proper fit off the rack, and forum consensus is that BARE's size chart is accurate.
cons: At about $600 US it is one of the most expensive 5mm suits sold, roughly double a Henderson Thermoprene Pro.
Several owners, including working divemasters, report back-zipper failures within 20–25 dives, sometimes on a warranty replacement suit as well.
Some divers dismiss the infrared-mineral claims as marketing, since the warmth benefit is hard to separate from the suit's tight NST construction.

items.name: Fourth Element Proteus II 5mm
items.blurb: The benchmark for warmth in a full-stretch diving suit.
items.review: The Proteus II is built on the observation that most of a wetsuit's heat loss is water exchange, not conduction, so Fourth Element attacked the openings. The neck gets a double-layer Hydrolock seal with a second overhead closure, the wrists get Glideskin, and the ankles get internal PU dams; combined with double-glued, blind-stitched and taped seams, water that gets in tends to stay and warm up. Hexcore lining on the torso holds insulating air pockets, Thermoflex covers the rest, and the result is a 5mm rated for prolonged diving at 19°C and up that testers have worn comfortably at 18°C (64°F). The neoprene is limestone-derived and glued with water-based Aqua-X. The cost of all this sealing is friction: the neck is genuinely punishing when new, and owner threads are full of cylinder-stretching remedies and warnings to expect a fight on land that disappears at depth. Budget around $420 US and some patience in the first week. It suits divers doing long, cool-water dives who will tolerate a slow donning routine for measurably less flushing; divers who prioritize easy on-off should look at the Henderson or Aquaflex instead.

specs.Material: Stretch neoprene with recycled thermal lining
specs.Seams: Glued and blind-stitched, taped stress points
specs.Lining: Thermal plush lining with Hydrolock neck seal
specs.Neck seal: double-layer Hydrolock with a second overhead closure
specs.Adhesive: water-based Aqua-X glue on limestone-derived neoprene

pros: The sealing package is the most complete in its class: a double-layer Hydrolock neck with overhead closure, Glideskin wrists and internal PU ankle dams, which together nearly stop the flushing that makes most 5mm suits feel thin.
Hexcore lining on the torso traps insulating air pockets while Thermoflex covers the limbs; testers report staying comfortable at 18°C (64°F) in what is nominally a 19°C-and-up suit.
Seams are double glued, blind stitched and then taped, so the construction backs up the seals rather than leaking around them.
Limestone-based neoprene laminated with water-based Aqua-X glue, for divers who care how the rubber is made.
cons: The Hydrolock neck seal is notoriously tight out of the box; forum threads describe it as choking, with owners stretching it over a dive cylinder for two days before it became wearable.
Donning is slow: the wrist, ankle and two-stage neck seals mean this is not a suit you throw on between shallow boat dives.
Because the seals grip harder than the body panels, sizing is awkward, and some divers between sizes cannot find a combination where both the suit and the neck fit.

items.name: Scubapro Everflex Yulex 5/4mm
items.blurb: Ultra-flexible plant-based neoprene that feels barely there underwater.
items.review: The Everflex Yulex replaces petroleum neoprene with Yulex, a foam made from FSC-certified natural rubber, and Scubapro claims its dive-grade formulation resists the compression that robs ordinary suits of warmth at depth. The rest of the suit is conventional and well specified: 5mm torso with 4mm limbs, blind-stitched seams, Blue Diamond Span fleece across the core that drains rather than soaks, and linings spun from about 45 recycled bottles. Hardware is YKK throughout, with a brass-slider rear zip and brass KA sliders on the ankle zips. It carries CE Class B certification for 10–18°C, and in a reviewed UK dive at 13°C it held up for 50 minutes. The trade-offs are stretch and price: Yulex gives up elasticity to neoprene, the fit is snug and reportedly tightens after salt-water use, and the internal seals bite on thicker forearms and calves. At $586 US it is nearly double the neoprene Everflex it resembles. Worth it if eliminating neoprene matters to you and you dive genuinely cool water; otherwise the money buys a lot of conventional suit.

specs.Certification: CE certified Class B dive suit
specs.Ankle zips: YKK zippered ankles with brass KA sliders
specs.Recycled content: interior and exterior linings spun from about 45 recycled plastic bottles per suit
specs.Abrasion protection: reinforced seat and shoulder linings plus printed knee and shoulder pads

pros: The only dive-specific Yulex suit on the market: the FSC-certified natural-rubber foam is formulated to resist compression at depth, so it loses less insulation below 20 m than standard neoprene of the same thickness.
CE certified Class B for 10–18°C water, and a magazine tester stayed warm through a 50-minute UK dive at 13°C.
Hardware is specified for salt water: a rear YKK zipper with brass slider and long webbing puller, plus YKK ankle zips with brass KA sliders that make donning over dive boots straightforward.
Blue Diamond Span fleece on the torso drains water rather than holding it, and the linings are made from roughly 45 recycled bottles per suit.
The Pure Design panel layout uses fewer seams than a conventional 5mm, which helps shoulder mobility despite the stiffer rubber.
cons: At $586 US it costs roughly twice Scubapro's own Definition line, and forum consensus is the premium is for the plant-based rubber, not extra warmth.
Yulex stretches less than good neoprene: the suit fits snug from day one, owners report it feels tighter after repeated salt-water dives, and several wish they had sized up.
The internal wrist and ankle seals run tight on thicker forearms and calves, with one reviewer describing reduced circulation to the hands.

items.name: Henderson Thermoprene Pro 5mm
items.blurb: A do-everything 5mm workhorse backed by a 10-year seam warranty.
items.review: The Thermoprene Pro is Henderson's argument that comfort sells wetsuits, built on a 250% stretch neoprene that replaces the 180% fabric of the original Thermoprene. In practice that means donning a dry 5mm takes a fraction of the effort of a conventional suit, and a wet one goes back on for the afternoon boat without wrestling. Henderson minimizes the seam count and double-glues and blind-stitches what remains, lines the interior with Aqua Silk, and closes it with a self-sealing back zipper. The size run is the real differentiator: men's XS to 6XL with short and tall variants, patterned and fit-tested in the USA against five decades of Henderson sizing data. At roughly $280–395 US it undercuts every other suit in this group, and the warranty (5 years on materials, 10 on the zipper) is longer than suits costing twice as much. The caveat is longevity: forum accounts of separating seams and delaminating nylon on Henderson suits are common enough to take seriously, and very soft neoprene inherently compresses and scuffs faster. As a warm, easy-wearing travel and vacation suit it is hard to fault at the price; divers logging 150 dives a year should weigh the durability reports.

specs.Stretch: 250% elongation, versus 180% for the original Thermoprene
specs.Warranty: 5 years materials and workmanship, 10 years zipper, 1 year head-to-toe
specs.Sizes: men's XS–6XL including short and tall cuts (around 20 options)
specs.Pattern development: designed, patterned, cut and fit-tested in the USA before production

pros: The 250% stretch neoprene (up from 180% in the original Thermoprene) makes this one of the easiest 5mm suits to don and doff, including wet.
The size run is the widest in its class: men's XS through 6XL with short and tall cuts, so divers other suits ignore can get a real fit.
Seam count is deliberately minimized and every seam is double glued and blind stitched, which limits water seepage without taped-seam stiffness.
The warranty is unusually long for the price: 5 years on materials and workmanship and 10 years on the zipper.
Street prices around $280–395 US make it the value pick against $420–600 competitors.
cons: Henderson's durability record divides forums: owners report seams separating on nearly new suits and outer nylon delaminating from the neoprene on older Henderson models.
Some divers find the cut tight through the chest and shoulders relative to the size chart, despite the stretchy fabric.
Very soft, high-stretch neoprene trades some robustness for comfort; it marks and compresses more readily than stiffer suits.

items.name: Aqua Lung Aquaflex 5mm
items.blurb: Four-way stretch and Canadian-style seals for stretch, comfort and warmth.
items.review: The Aquaflex has been Aqua Lung's mainstream dive suit for years, and its pitch is unchanged: four-way stretch neoprene with about three times the elasticity of standard rubber, so a 5mm stops being a wrestling match. The stretch is not just convenience; a suit that stays snug against the body flushes less water, which is where most of a 5mm's warmth actually goes. Aqua Lung backs that with skin-to-skin wrist and ankle seals finished without edge tape (tape kills stretch), liquid-rubber sealed seams, and an extra insulation panel over the kidneys. Two details show the dive focus: a silicone traction pad on the left forearm stops a wrist computer spinning once the neoprene compresses at depth, and the arms and legs are pre-bent to cut fatigue. Owners report multi-hour comfort around 20°C (68°F) with a hood, which matches its warm-to-temperate remit. The cut is the main complaint, running slim through the forearms and chest, and the men's S–3XL run offers no short or tall options, so broad-shouldered or long-torsoed divers should try before buying. It is the sensible mid-price pick for divers who value easy donning over maximum cold-water performance.

specs.Lining: Nylon jersey with Canadian wrist and ankle seals
specs.Seals: skin-to-skin wrist and ankle seals on the 5mm and 7mm models, with no stretch-killing edge tape
specs.Computer pad: silicone traction pad on the left forearm to stop a dive computer rotating as the neoprene compresses
specs.Kidney panel: additional insulating material across the kidney area
specs.Anatomic cut: pre-bent arms and legs to reduce strain at depth

pros: The four-way stretch neoprene has roughly three times the stretch of standard neoprene, and owners consistently cite easy on-off as the reason they bought it.

Details are dive-specific rather than borrowed from surf suits: a silicone pad on the left forearm keeps a wrist computer from rotating once the neoprene compresses, and an extra insulation panel covers the kidneys.
Wrist and ankle seals on the 5mm are finished skin-to-skin with no edge tape, since tape kills stretch, and the seams are sealed with liquid rubber against seepage.
Pre-bent arms and legs reduce fatigue on long dives; one owner reported staying warm past the two-hour mark at 20°C (68°F) with a hood and rash guard.
cons: The cut runs slim through the forearms, wrists and chest, and divers with muscular builds report the sleeves feeling tight a size up from their usual.
Men's sizing is a straight S–3XL run with no short or tall cuts, unlike the Henderson or BARE size charts.
It is sold in a single dark colorway, which makes suits easy to confuse on a crowded dive boat.

items.name: Cressi Castoro 5mm
items.blurb: Italian-engineered double-lined warmth at an unbeatable entry price.
items.review: The Castoro is Cressi's budget workhorse: 5 mm double-lined Nylon II neoprene, a YKK back zip with an Aquastop under-flap, and 2 mm smooth-skin seals at the wrists and ankles. Those seals are the point — once water gets past them it stays put, and owners regularly report staying comfortable in 15-16°C (about 60°F) water where other entry-level 5 mm suits flush. The trade-off is donning: the tight cuffs fight you, particularly when the suit is dry, and the Italian sizing runs a size slimmer than North American brands. Pre-shaped legs and anti-wear panels on the knees and shins handle shore entries, and a pull leash on the zip makes solo closing workable. Long-term durability is the weak spot — the neoprene compresses with use, and one owner measured roughly 3 mm left after two years of regular diving. At a street price under $200 it undercuts the big-brand 5 mm suits by $150 or more. Order a size up, treat it as a warm starter suit for temperate water, and budget to replace it sooner than a premium suit.

specs.Reinforcements: Anti-wear inserts on knees and shins; pre-shaped legs
specs.Sizes: Men's M/3 to XXXL/7; separate Castoro Lady women's cut
specs.Zip aid: Pull leash on the YKK back zip for solo closing

pros: The 2 mm smooth-skin Aquastop wrist and ankle seals nearly stop flushing — owners report that water which gets in stays in and warms up rather than cycling through
Warm beyond its price: divers report comfort in 15-16°C (about 60°F) water, where most sub-$200 5 mm suits flush cold
YKK back zip with an Aquastop under-flap and a pull leash makes solo donning and closing workable
Anti-wear knee and shin inserts plus pre-shaped legs hold up to rocky shore entries
Street price around $170-200, roughly $150 under the mainstream 5 mm suits
cons: The tight smooth-skin cuffs and ankle seals make the suit a genuine fight to pull on, especially when dry
Cressi's Italian sizing runs slimmer than North American brands — most divers need to order one size up
The neoprene compresses with use; one owner measured roughly 3 mm remaining of the original 5 mm after two years of regular diving

items.name: Bare Nixie Ultra 5mm Women's
items.blurb: A women's-specific 5mm with graphene warmth cut for a true female fit.
items.review: BARE builds the Nixie Ultra around two things: a women-specific pattern and the OMNIRED lining, which weaves 13 thermo-reactive minerals into the interior fabric to reflect body heat back as infrared. In the water that reads as a 5 mm that runs warmer than its thickness, and divers who move between plain-lined suits and this one consistently notice the difference on longer dives. The size run is the other real asset: 2 through 14 with tall and plus variants of most sizes, cut by a women's design team rather than scaled down from a men's block. Donning is easier than most 5 mm suits thanks to zippers at the collar, wrists and ankles, and the Glideskin collar seals without the raw-neck feel of a stitched edge. Full-stretch neoprene throughout keeps shoulder mobility close to a 3 mm suit. The price is the catch — around $430 puts it at the top of the 5 mm band — and the tall and plus sizes sell through at US retailers. Seams carry a 7-year warranty against the 2-year cover on the rest, which says something about where BARE expects it to fail — nowhere soon.

specs.Zip: Back zip with collar zip and ankle zips
specs.Collar: Glideskin smooth-skin sealing collar with its own zipper
specs.Ancillary zippers: Zippers at collar, wrists and ankles for donning and doffing
specs.Sizes: Women's 2-14 including tall (04T, 06T, 08T) and plus (06+, 08+, 10+, 12+) fits
specs.Warranty: 2 years on materials and workmanship; seams covered for 7 years

pros: The OMNIRED interior weaves 13 thermo-reactive minerals into the lining to reflect body heat back as infrared — divers rate it noticeably warmer than plain-lined 5 mm suits
Zippers at the collar, wrists and ankles cut the usual 5 mm donning wrestle to a minimum
Patterned by BARE's women's design team with tall and plus variants of most sizes (04T, 08+, 12+), so long torsos and wider hips get a real cut, not a scaled men's suit
The Glideskin collar seals against flushing without chafing the neck
Seams are warrantied for 7 years, well past the 2-year cover on the rest of the suit
cons: At around $430 it sits at the top of the 5 mm price band
The tall and plus sizes drift in and out of stock at US retailers, so the fit advantage can mean waiting
One colorway — black — with no lighter option for divers who overheat topside in the sun

items.name: Scubapro Definition Steamer 5mm
items.blurb: A body-mapped 5mm that fits like a glove and insulates like a flagship.
items.review: The Definition Steamer is Scubapro's mid-range 5 mm, and its case rests on tailoring rather than gadgetry. The Body Map System uses a 3D cut with an Ultraspan chest panel and Stretchtec fabric through the body, sides, arms and legs, so the stretch lands where back-zip suits usually bind at the shoulders and chest. The neoprene is X-Foam, Scubapro's petroleum-free limestone formulation, which keeps dry weight to 2.15 kg and insulates efficiently for the thickness; the suit carries a CE Class B rating for 10-18°C (50-64.5°F) water. Seamless one-piece arm and leg panels end in double-zippered seals, which makes it one of the quicker 5 mm back-zips to get into. Oversized abrasion pads on the shoulders, knees and elbows absorb BCD and ladder wear. The recurring complaint is fit: the Euro cut runs slim, and forum threads are full of divers who needed one or even two sizes above their US size. Divers comparing it against Scubapro's own Everflex line generally conclude the Definition does the job at a meaningfully lower price. If the size chart works for your build, it is a durable temperate-water suit; if not, no feature list will fix the fit.

specs.Protection: Abrasion-resistant pads on shoulders, knees and elbows
specs.Sizes: Men's S-2XL including short (LS, XLS) and tall (MT, LT) cuts

pros: Body Map System tailoring puts an Ultraspan panel on the chest and Stretchtec fabric on the body, sides, arms and legs — stretch exactly where a back-zip suit normally binds
X-Foam limestone-based neoprene is 100% petroleum-free and keeps the dry weight to 2.15 kg, light for a 5 mm in a travel bag
Double-zippered wrist and ankle seals over seamless one-piece arm and leg panels make donning quicker than most back-zip 5 mm suits
Oversized abrasion pads on shoulders, knees and elbows take BCD and boat-ladder wear instead of the neoprene
Owners report Scubapro suits surviving years of regular travel diving without seam failures
cons: Scubapro's Euro cut runs slim — forum consensus is one to two sizes smaller than US brands, so try it on or size up
One colorway per cut (black/blue men's, black/turquoise women's), with no neutral all-black men's option

items.name: Beuchat Focea Comfort 6
items.blurb: Sixth generation of Beuchat's flagship one-piece, in 5 mm ultra-flexible Elaskin neoprene with Fireskin thermal lining and the patented Water Dam seal system. The 5 mm men's version covers roughly 18-26 degree C water.
items.review: The Focea Comfort 6 is Beuchat's answer to the question of why a 5 mm suit has to feel like one. The collar version is assembled from 44 pre-formed panels in 20 different grades of Elaskin neoprene, and the effect in the water is a suit that reviewers say bends more like a 3 mm. Warmth comes from a hydrophobic Fireskin panel across the chest, semi-dry double seals at the wrists and ankles, and Beuchat's patented Water Dam baffle under the metal-slider G-Lock back zip; the 5 mm version rates CE thermal class B, the class a plain 7 mm normally earns. Donning is unusually easy for the thickness — zippers at the wrists and ankles, plus a small relief zip at the throat for surface intervals, all with neoprene-reinforced zip ends. Supratex 2 knee pads and polyurethane shield panels on the shoulders, seat and legs handle the abrasion points. The neoprene is limestone-derived rather than petroleum-based. The caveats are French: sizing follows European proportions, so US divers should work strictly from the measurement chart, and the hooded variant drops to 40 panels in 14 grades, trading some of the tailoring for the built-in hood. As temperate-water 5 mm suits go, this is the one built around pattern-making rather than marketing.

specs.Stretch zones: Flex System 2, Free Flex Zone shoulders
specs.Certification: CE thermal class B in the 5 mm version — the rating a plain 7 mm usually earns
specs.Panel count: 44 panels in 20 neoprene grades (collar version); hooded version uses 40 panels in 14 grades
specs.Versions: Men's and women's cuts, each offered with attached hood or plain collar
