The 5 mm version carries a CE class B thermal rating — the class a plain 7 mm usually earns — helped by the Fireskin chest panel and the Water Dam baffle under the G-Lock back zip
Wrist and ankle zippers plus a small neck-relief zip make it one of the easiest 5 mm suits to get on and off; every zip end is reinforced with neoprene to stop tearing
Supratex 2 knee pads and shield panels on the shoulders, seat and legs take boat-ladder and rock wear
cons: Beuchat's European sizing assumes a taller, slimmer frame than US charts — measure against the chart rather than guessing your usual letter size
Choosing the attached-hood version drops the construction to 40 panels in 14 neoprene grades, so some of the fit advantage goes with it

items.name: Bare Velocity Ultra 2.0 5mm
items.blurb: Bare's updated full-stretch 5mm gives travelling divers a warm, easy-donning suit for temperate reefs and deep cenote days. The 2.0 revision keeps the Omnired heat-reflective lining and sharpens the anatomical fit.
items.review: The Velocity Ultra 2.0 is BARE's mid-line 5 mm, and its main idea is zoning: three different full-stretch neoprenes placed around the body in what BARE calls Progressive Full-Stretch construction, so the panels that need to move, move. The fit reputation follows from that — owners describe it as glove-like, and one 187 cm, 75 kg diver reported no gaps or flushing straight off the rack. Warmth comes from the OMNIRED torso lining, which uses 13 thermo-reactive minerals to reflect body heat back as infrared, aimed at 10-18°C water. Sixteen sizes in Black, including eight short and tall variants, mean most builds avoid a custom order; the Blue and Lava colorways cover far fewer sizes. Flatseam stitching keeps the seams stretching with the panels, the double-glued Secure-Lock construction keeps water out of them, and Protekt panels cover the knees. The complaints are consistent: the back zip runs shorter than on comparable Scubapro and Henderson suits, so closing it solo is awkward and at least one diver tore a zip forcing it, and the $464.95 list price sits above most of the mid-range field. The 7-year seam warranty softens the price somewhat. This is the suit for divers who care about fit first and are willing to pay for the pattern work.

specs.Construction: Progressive Full-Stretch — three full-stretch neoprene types zoned around the body, double-glued with Secure-Lock seams
specs.Interior: OMNIRED thermal lining with 13 thermo-reactive minerals woven into the torso
specs.Warranty: 2 years on materials and workmanship; seams covered for 7 years

The OMNIRED torso lining uses 13 thermo-reactive minerals to reflect body heat back as infrared, extending comfort in 10-18°C water
Sixteen sizes in the Black colorway, including short and tall fits (MLS, LS, XLS, 2XLS, MT, MLT, LT, XLT), so most builds get a proper off-the-rack fit
Flatseam stitching keeps the seams as stretchy as the panels, and the double-glued Secure-Lock construction blocks water entry through them

items.name: Fourth Element Xenos ARC 5mm
items.blurb: The current generation of Fourth Element's stretch suit swaps petroleum neoprene for Ocena plant-based natural rubber. A supple 5mm aimed at liveaboard weeks and multi-dive days.
items.review: Fourth Element replaced the neoprene in its liveaboard suit with Ocena, a natural-rubber compound with recycled tyre content that certifies at 82% bio-based. The certificate is not the point: in the company's blind trials over 60-100 dives, staff divers picked the Ocena suit for comfort, and it compressed less than the neoprene version — relevant because a 5 mm suit that has crushed thinner no longer insulates like one at 25 m. The build targets multi-dive days, with low-friction fast-dry linings in the arms and legs so the suit goes back on close to dry between dives, and a dual-layer thermal lining concentrating insulation in the torso. Triple glued-and-blindstitched seams, a Glideskin neck, Hydrolock wrist seals and internal silicone ankle seals keep flushing down for a back-zip suit. Short, Standard and Tall cuts across XS-3XL fit most bodies off the rack. The catch is the invoice: at $615 (£399) it costs half again to twice what proven 5 mm dive suits sell for, and the durability evidence so far is the manufacturer's own. For a diver logging heavy annual volume in temperate and cool tropical water, the donning speed and slower compression earn the premium; at 20 dives a year the maths is harder.

Triple glued-and-blindstitched seams plus Glideskin neck, Hydrolock wrist and internal silicone ankle seals keep flushing low for a rear-zip suit
Short, Standard and Tall cuts across XS-3XL in men's and women's patterns cover long or short torsos without alteration
cons: At $615 (£399) it sits at the top of the 5 mm market; established dive-brand 5 mm suits sell for half to two-thirds of that
The compression and comfort data comes from the manufacturer's own trials — the material is too new for independent long-term wear reports
Short and Tall options are offered in most, but not all, sizes

items.name: O'Neill Epic 4/3mm
items.blurb: O'Neill's long-running 4/3 fills the awkward gap between a 3mm and a 5mm for divers who run cold. A 4mm core with 3mm limbs keeps warmth where it counts and stretch where you move.
items.review: The Epic is O'Neill's mid-range surf suit, and divers keep buying it because it does the temperate-water job at roughly half the price of a dedicated dive suit. The 100% UltraFlex neoprene stretches more than most dive rubber, so the back-zip entry with pull leash works for wide shoulders, and donning takes seconds rather than a struggle. Warmth comes from FluidFlex Firewall thermal panels in the chest and back plus wind-blocking smoothskin, while the double Super Seal neck and glued-and-blindstitched seams keep flushing modest for a rear zipper. Rental operations use Epics because the rubber holds up to daily abuse, and Krypto Armor knee pads take kneeling on sand or swim steps. The trade-off is the same softness that makes it easy to put on: surf neoprene compresses more under pressure than dive neoprene, so below about 20 m it runs colder than the 4/3 rating implies. There is no seat or shoulder reinforcement for tank straps and ladders, and the warranty runs one year. As a travel and shoulder-season scuba suit for water above roughly 15°C (59°F) it is a sound buy; as a primary cold-water dive suit it is not.

specs.Zip: Covert blackout back zip, double-seal neck
specs.Lining: FluidFlex Firewall thermal panels in chest and back

pros: At €239.95 list (US street prices similar) it costs roughly half of any dedicated dive suit in this group
100% UltraFlex neoprene stretches more than dive-grade rubber, so the back-zip entry works even with wide shoulders or stiff joints
Rental fleets run the Epic because the rubber tolerates abuse; the covert blackout back zip and Krypto Armor knee pads survive boat decks
FluidFlex Firewall panels in the chest and back add insulation exactly where a rear zipper loses it, and the smoothskin exterior blocks wind on the surface interval
cons: It is cut and compounded for surfing: the soft neoprene compresses harder at depth than dive-grade rubber, so on a 25 m dive it insulates like a thinner suit than the 4/3 label suggests
No seat or shoulder abrasion panels — knee pads are the only reinforcement, so tank straps and boat ladders wear on bare jersey
One-year warranty is short next to Henderson's five years on materials and ten on the zipper

thickness_groups.label: 7 mm — cold water
thickness_groups.article_intro: 7 mm is about as far as a wetsuit sensibly goes — kelp forests, quarries, temperate reefs, shoulder-season diving in the low teens. It only works if it seals: open wrists, ankles and a loose neck let cold water flush through and strip away the warm layer your body just heated, which is why the better suits use smooth-skin or glued cuffs and a snug collar. Below about 10°C most divers switch to a semi-dry or a drysuit rather than pile on more neoprene.
tips: Sealed or smooth-skin cuffs and a snug neck are non-negotiable at 7 mm — an unsealed suit flushes and you'll get cold fast.
Expect to add weight: thick neoprene is buoyant, so plan your weighting and check trim on the first dive.
A semi-dry with glide-skin seals holds a warmer water layer than a plain 7 mm — worth it if you dive cold often.

items.name: Henderson Thermoprene Pro 7mm
items.blurb: The default-choice cold-water suit: 250% super-stretch neoprene that dives above its price bracket.
items.review: Henderson's Thermoprene Pro takes the standard Thermoprene recipe and raises the neoprene's stretch from 180% to 250%, which in a 7 mm suit is the difference between a wrestling match and a normal morning. It also holds its shape wet, so the second dive does not start with a bagged-out suit. Construction is deliberate: double glued-and-blindstitched seams, a #10 YKK back zipper with a stainless slider and leash, Aqua Silk lining on the interior panels and ergonomic Duratex knee pads. The size run is the strongest argument — XS-3XL plus nine short and tall cuts and 4XL through 6XL, all cut and sewn in the USA — so divers who never fit off-the-rack suits usually fit this one. Warranty cover is unusually long at ten years on the zipper and five on materials and workmanship. Two complaints recur: the fit runs small against the chart, with divers sizing up after sleeves bunched at the wrist, and the extended sizes are only made in the Black/Heather Blue trim. Priced well under the premium 7 mm suits, it is the value pick of the cold-water field.

specs.Material: Thermoprene Pro super-stretch neoprene (up to 250% stretch)
specs.Seams: Glued and blindstitched, spot-taped stress points
specs.Lining: Nylon plush interior with glideskin seals
specs.Sizes: XS-3XL plus short/tall cuts (MS, MT, ML, LS, LT, XLS, XLT, 2XLS, 2XLT) and 4XL-6XL
specs.Warranty: 10 years zipper, 5 years materials and workmanship, 1 year head-to-toe
specs.Origin: Designed, patterned, cut and sewn in the USA

pros: 250% neoprene stretch — up from 180% in the standard Thermoprene — makes a 7 mm suit that dons like a much thinner one
Around 19 sizes from XS to 6XL including nine short and tall cuts, one of the widest fit ranges in diving
Warranty runs ten years on the zipper and five on materials and workmanship, several times the industry norm
#10 YKK back zipper with stainless steel slider and leash shrugs off grit and hard pulls
Holds its shape when wet — owners report it does not bag out between dives the way softer suits can
cons: Runs small against the size chart; divers report a nominal Large bunching at the wrist and forearm and refusing to zip, forcing a size up
The extended sizes (short/tall and 4XL-6XL) only come in the Black/Heather Blue trim — Black/Heather Gray stops at 3XL

items.name: BARE Reactive 7mm
items.blurb: The warmest suit in BARE's range, with graphene infrared lining that actively kicks back your body heat.
items.review: The Reactive is BARE's warmest wetsuit, and the mechanism is the lining rather than extra millimetres: Omnired fabric woven with 13 thermoreactive minerals runs the full length of the suit, converting body heat to infrared and reflecting it back. In 13°C (55°F) test water the full-coverage lining held warmth in areas where suits with torso-only thermal panels showed cold spots. The Elastek full-stretch neoprene keeps a 7 mm suit manageable to don, helped by ankle zips, and the 53 cm S-Lock2 back zipper uses PU-coated tape with a skin-to-skin double internal flap to limit water entry. Sixteen sizes from S to 4XL, including short and tall cuts, plus a velcro-adjustable collar with a booklet-style cover to fine-tune the neck seal. It is priced like a flagship at $615-685, the top of the 7 mm bracket. The fit is athletic: the size chart puts many divers between sizes, and there are reports of the collar edge rubbing the neck raw across multi-day trips. Warranty is two years on materials and workmanship — fine, but a fraction of Henderson's cover on a cheaper suit.

specs.Material: Elastek full-stretch neoprene with Graphene Omnired laminate
specs.Zip: S-Lock2 offset-tooth back zip with PU-coated tape
specs.Sizes: 16 sizes S-4XL including short and tall cuts (S, M, MT, ML, MLS, MLT, L, LS, LT, XL, XLS, XLT, 2XL, 2XLS, 3XL, 4XL)

pros: The Omnired thermal lining with 13 thermoreactive minerals runs through the entire suit, not just a torso panel — in 13°C (55°F) test water it stayed warm in zones where partially lined suits went cold
Extra-long 53 cm S-Lock2 back zipper with PU-coated tape and a skin-to-skin double internal flap cuts water entry at the suit's weakest point
Elastek full-stretch neoprene plus ankle zips make a 7 mm suit that goes on without a fight
16 sizes from S to 4XL including short and tall cuts, with seamless underarm panels that do not bind at full arm extension
cons: At $615-685 it is the most expensive 7 mm in this group
The cut is athletic and tight — BARE's own chart lands many divers between sizes, and owners often recommend a different size than the chart does
Reports of the collar edge chafing the neck raw over consecutive diving days

items.name: Waterproof W7 7mm
items.blurb: A semi-dry-grade 7mm loaded with seals, pockets and Kevlar knees for divers who want a suit that lasts.
items.review: Waterproof builds the W7 like a semi-dry: smoothskin flaps overlap on both sides of the back zipper, the wrists and ankles carry double seals, and owners consistently report almost no flushing. The 7 mm microcell limestone neoprene stretches four ways and arrives pre-bent at the elbows and knees, so the suit fights you less at depth than its thickness implies. It is also the only suit in this group with real working features: two flexible thigh pockets hold an SMB and a spare mask, and two anti-slip anchors keep a wrist computer's strap from migrating as the neoprene compresses. Wear points get heat-moulded aramid knee pads set in Duratex and a Duratex seat panel for ladders and tank benches. The men's range runs sixteen sizes including tall and widened cuts, but the chart runs small — a 175 cm, 75 kg diver reported needing an XL after the L refused to close comfortably. The zipper is a vertical back zip rather than a horizontal dry-zip, so it needs a buddy and stops short of true semi-dry sealing. At around $551 it lands between the Henderson and the BARE on price, and for cold, repetitive boat diving without a drysuit it is the most complete 7 mm here.

specs.Seams: Glued and blindstitched with WaterDam spine baffle
specs.Zip: Back zip plus sealed wrist and ankle zips
specs.Sizes: 16 men's sizes: XS-XXL plus tall cuts (S/t-XXL/t) and widened M/+, L/+, 3XL/t+
specs.Knee pads: Heat-moulded aramid (Kevlar) fabric surrounded by Duratex
specs.Pockets: Two flexible thigh pockets sized for a spare mask, SMB or tables
specs.Extras: Two anti-slip computer strap anchors; Duratex seat panel

pros: Semi-dry sealing on a wetsuit chassis: smoothskin flaps overlap both sides of the back zipper and the wrists and ankles get double seals, and owners report almost no flushing
The only suit in this class with two thigh pockets that actually swallow an SMB and a spare mask, plus two anti-slip anchors that stop a computer strap sliding on compressed neoprene
3D-sculpted, pre-bent arms and legs in 4-way-stretch microcell limestone neoprene — soft and easy to don for a 7 mm
Wear points are armoured: aramid knee pads set in Duratex and a Duratex seat panel for boat ladders and tank benches
cons: The size chart runs small — a 175 cm, 75 kg (5'9", 165 lb) diver reported the L would not close comfortably and needed an XL; size up if between measurements
The double zipper seal is a vertical back zip, not a horizontal dry-zip, so it still needs a buddy to close and cannot quite match a true semi-dry
At around $551 it costs more than the Henderson while one owner still found it colder than expected in 20-21°C (69-70°F) water

items.name: Scubapro Everflex Steamer 7/5mm
items.blurb: A body-mapped 7/5 steamer that keeps thick warmth over the core and thinner stretch where you move.
items.review: The Everflex Steamer splits its thickness deliberately: 7 mm through the core, 5 mm in the arms and legs, so the torso stays insulated while the limbs keep enough bend for reaching valves. The neoprene is Scubapro's X-Foam, assembled with a solvent-free water-based glue, and the panel layout trims seam count under what the brand calls its Pure Design Concept. What separates it from most back-zip steamers is the sealing: Glideskin at the neck, wrists and ankles plus a sealed diagonal rear zipper, which is why owners routinely describe it behaving like a semi-dry. It carries a CE Class A rating for 7-12°C (45-54°F) water, and real-world reports back that up — divers log it comfortable at 12°C with a hood and gloves. The Diamond Span interior lining drains water and slides on more easily than open-cell alternatives. The trade-offs are cost, around $550 new, and a zipper seal whose stitching wears with heavy use. Scubapro now steers buyers to the Yulex-rubber successor, so this version is effectively run-out stock — a bargaining point if your size is still on the shelf.

specs.Seals: Glideskin smooth-skin seals at neck, wrists and ankles
specs.Sizes: Men's S-2XL including short and tall cuts (LS, MT, XLT)

pros: The 7 mm core with 5 mm arms and legs keeps torso insulation where it counts; owners report comfortable dives at 10-12°C (50-54°F) and one logged 37 m (123 ft) in 12°C water with hood and gloves.
Glideskin seals at neck, wrists, ankles and behind the zipper cut flushing to the point that owners treat it as a semi-dry substitute.
The Pure Design panel layout uses fewer seams than a conventional cut, with double-blind stitching outside and single-blind stitching inside.
Assembled with solvent-free water-based Aqua glue instead of conventional solvent adhesives.
cons: The stitching on the zipper seal is a wear point — one long-term owner reported it fraying enough to need seam glue after heavy use.
At roughly $550 new it costs about twice as much as a Cressi Fast.
Scubapro's current catalogue has moved to the Everflex Yulex version, so this X-Foam suit is sold from remaining retailer stock and size runs are getting patchy.

items.name: Aqua Lung Aquaflex 7mm
items.blurb: A supple 4-way-stretch 7mm with dedicated neck and wrist seals that punch above its mid-range price.
items.review: The Aquaflex's pitch is simple: build the whole suit, not just accent panels, from four-way stretch neoprene that Aqua Lung rates at three times the stretch of standard foam. In practice that means a 7 mm suit you can pull on unassisted and a fit that conforms rather than bridges, which matters for warmth because a 7 mm suit that fits loosely is a 7 mm suit that flushes. The 7 mm version adds overlapping smooth-skin seals at the wrists and ankles, and the back zipper closes over a three-way seal backed by spine and kidney padding, so the usual cold stripe up the back never develops. Seams are glued and blind-stitched; knees get Powertex abrasion panels, and the cuff edges are finished with an X-Tend treatment instead of glue-on tape that eventually lifts. Divers who use it in cold quarries report staying warm where other 7 mm suits let them chill. The catch is sizing: owners consistently report needing a size up from their thinner Aquaflex, so try before buying if you can. At $489 it is priced as a premium suit, roughly $240 above budget 7 mm competition.

specs.Lining: Plush thermal lining with neck and wrist seals
specs.Seals: Overlapping smooth-skin wrist and ankle seals (7mm-specific) plus three-way back-zip seal
specs.Back padding: Spine and kidney padding behind the zipper
specs.Cuff finish: X-Tend stretch-preserving cuffs, no edge tape

pros: Four-way stretch neoprene with a claimed three times the stretch of standard foam makes a 7 mm suit that dons and squeezes down like a thinner one.
The three-way back-zip seal with spine and kidney padding closes off the usual back-zip cold channel; testers found it does not pump water when finning.
The 7 mm version gets overlapping wrist and ankle seals that limit exchange without zippers to corrode or jam.
X-Tend cuffs replace glued-on edge tape, removing the classic failure point where tape peels after a season.
cons: Sizing is inconsistent across thicknesses — owners who wear one size in the 3 mm report needing a size larger in the 7 mm, so the chart is only a starting point.
At $489 on Aqua Lung's own store it costs nearly double a Cressi Fast for the same thickness.

items.name: Cressi Fast 7mm
items.blurb: A budget-friendly one-piece 7mm that delivers honest cold-water warmth without the premium markup.
items.review: The Fast is Cressi's price-led 7 mm suit, and the savings come from material choice, not corner-cutting on hardware. The body is conventional double-lined 7 mm neoprene — stiffer to pull on than superstretch, but it compresses less at depth, which means more of the rated thickness is still insulating you at 25 m. Cressi limits the stretch neoprene (Ultraspan) to the arms and calves, where range of motion actually matters for reaching valves and finning. Sealing is handled by a YKK back zipper over a wide Glideskin underflap and a collar preformed at 120° to match the diving head position, closed with a wide hook-and-loop tab. Knees carry Tatex anti-abrasion reinforcement, and pre-bent legs reduce bunching behind the knee. Cressi rates it for 10-18°C (50-64°F) water, which matches owner experience with a hood added at the cold end. The known flaw is proportion: the torso runs short, so divers with long backs find it rides up. At around $250 against $450-600 rivals, it is the value pick of this group, with a shorter 24-month warranty as part of the deal.

specs.Material: High-stretch neoprene with smooth-skin chest and back panels
specs.Collar: 120° pre-angled anatomic neck with hook-and-loop closure
specs.Flex panels: Ultraspan stretch neoprene on arms and calves

pros: At roughly $250 it undercuts comparable 7 mm dive suits by $150-250 while keeping a YKK zipper and taped construction details.
The double-lined 7 mm neoprene resists compression at depth better than superstretch foams, so it keeps more of its insulation below 20 m.
The YKK back zip runs over a wide Glideskin underflap, and the 120° forward-angled collar closes with a broad hook-and-loop tab, keeping spine and neck flushing low for a budget suit.
Ultraspan panels on the arms and calves put the stretch where reach and finning need it, offsetting the stiffer body panels.
cons: The torso runs short against the size chart; taller divers report the suit riding up at the waist or pulling at the shoulders.
Only the arms and calves use stretch neoprene — the double-lined body takes noticeably more effort to don than a full-superstretch suit.
The 24-month warranty is short next to Henderson's five-year material cover.

items.name: BARE Nixie Ultra 7mm
items.blurb: A women's-specific cold-water 7mm designed around the female form, with infrared lining for real warmth.
items.review: The Nixie Ultra is BARE's women's cold-water suit, cut on a women-specific pattern across fourteen sizes — 2 through 14 with tall and plus variants, a grid most brands do not attempt at 7 mm. The insulation story is layered: 7 mm of full-stretch neoprene plus an OMNIRED interior that embeds 13 thermo-reactive minerals in the lining to reflect body heat back as infrared rather than letting it bleed into the water. Owners describe it as not feeling like a heavy 7 mm — the Progressive Full-Stretch construction grades panel stretch to where the body bends. A Glideskin collar seals the neck, the underarms are seamless to avoid chafe under a harness, and the 48 cm (19 in) back zipper makes the shoulder opening generous. The consistent warning from owners is fit: it runs small, and the arms are cut tight enough that sizing up is common advice. Long hair and the back zip do not mix without a tie-back. BARE covers it with a two-year warranty on materials and workmanship, and it sells in two colorways rather than the usual all-black-only.

specs.Zip: Back zip with collar-zip donning system
specs.Sizes: Women's 2-14 including tall (4T-8T) and plus (6+-12+) cuts

pros: The OMNIRED lining weaves 13 thermo-reactive minerals into the fabric to reflect escaping body heat back as infrared, supplementing the 7 mm of foam.
Fourteen women's sizes including tall and plus cuts — a wider size grid than almost any other 7 mm women's suit.
Progressive Full-Stretch construction keeps the suit mobile; owners say it moves like a much thinner suit rather than a board-stiff 7 mm.
The Glideskin collar and seamless underarm panels cut flushing at the neck and chafe under a BCD harness.
cons: It runs small, and the arms are the pinch point — divers with anything more than slim arms report a fight getting in, so consider sizing up.
The 48 cm back zip can catch long hair on the way up if it is not tied back.

items.name: Henderson Greenprene 7mm
items.blurb: A neoprene-free 7mm built from plant-based foam for divers who want cold-water warmth with a lighter footprint.
items.review: The Greenprene is the suit to look at if the environmental math of neoprene bothers you: the insulating foam contains no neoprene at all, built instead from deproteinized natural rubber blended with sugar cane, plant oils and oyster shell, and certified biobased under the USDA BioPreferred program. The laminates follow the same logic — Bio-Span inside and Aqua-Silk outside, both 88% recycled polyester spun from bottles. None of that would matter if it dived badly, and it does not: the foam is lighter than neoprene, stretchier than Henderson's own Thermoprene line, and long-term owners report suits still tight after 100-plus dives. Two physical quirks follow from the material. It floats harder than neoprene, so budget an extra kilogram or so of lead when you switch, and the glued-and-sewn seams lack liquid tape, so expect a brief flush on entry before the suit warms up. The rear zipper carries a pull-tab leash for solo donning. Henderson backs it with five years on materials and ten on the zipper, the longest warranty here, which takes some sting out of a $500-600 street price.

specs.Bio content: USDA BioPreferred-certified biobased foam with sugar cane, plant oil and oyster shell additives
specs.Laminates: Bio-Span interior (88% recycled polyester / 12% nylon), Aqua-Silk exterior (88% recycled polyester / 12% spandex)
specs.Warranty: 5 years materials and workmanship, 10 years zipper
specs.Sizes: Men's XS-3XL including tall cuts; women's 4-12 including talls

pros: The foam is 100% neoprene-free — deproteinized natural rubber with sugar cane, plant oil and oyster shell additives, certified biobased under the USDA BioPreferred program.
It weighs less than an equivalent neoprene 7 mm and owners report more stretch than Henderson's own Thermoprene and Aqua Lock lines, making donning easier than the thickness suggests.
Long-term forum reports have suits holding up well past 100 dives with no delamination.
The 5-year material / 10-year zipper warranty is the longest cover of any suit in this group.
Interior and exterior laminates are made from recycled bottles (88% recycled polyester), and the suit ships in recycled packaging with no plastic.
cons: The foam is more buoyant than neoprene — owners report adding lead to compensate after switching.
Seams are glued and sewn but not liquid-taped, so there is an initial flush of water before the suit settles.
Retail pricing runs roughly $500-600 for the 7 mm, a premium of about $100 over comparable neoprene suits.

items.name: Henderson Aqua Lock 7mm
items.blurb: Henderson's flagship semi-dry for genuinely cold water, built around interlocking seals that mate with matching Aqua Lock hoods, boots and gloves. A drysuit alternative for quarry and cold-coast divers.
items.review: The Aqua Lock is Henderson's attempt to get semi-dry performance out of a back-zip wetsuit, and the mechanism is the interesting part: the wrist, ankle and neck openings are built as two thin neoprene layers, and you roll the outer layer back over the cuff of a matching Aqua Lock glove or boot, trapping it between the layers against an internal silicone seal. Water exchange at the extremities drops to almost nothing — but only if you buy into the system, because the hood, gloves and boots that complete the seal are separate purchases. The seams get the same obsessive treatment, double-glued and blind-stitched with a liquid-tape barrier painted over the outside to close the stitch holes. A PK G-Lock water-resistant zipper handles the spine. The second signature feature is the QuikDry lining, a hydrophobic micro-fleece that Henderson claims — and owners confirm — dries in minutes, which changes the calculus on two-dive days in cold weather. Forum sentiment is blunt: not cheap, but repeatedly described by owners as the warmest wetsuit they have used, with the 5 mm keeping up with rivals' 7 mm suits. Budget accordingly; the 5 mm lists around $575 and the 7 mm sits above that before any of the matching accessories.

specs.Seals: Interlocking zipperless wrist, ankle and neck seals
specs.Lining: QuikDry hydrophobic micro-fleece interior; dries in minutes rather than hours
specs.System: Double-layer roll-back wrist, ankle and neck openings interlock with Aqua Lock hoods, gloves and boots (silicone-sealed)
specs.Seam finish: Outer seams coated with Aqua Lock liquid-tape waterproof barrier

pros: The zipperless double-layer wrist and ankle closures roll back to trap glove and boot cuffs between two neoprene layers, interlocking with Aqua Lock accessories into a near semi-dry seal.
Outer seams carry a liquid-tape barrier over double-glued blind stitching, sealing the stitch holes that flush on conventionally sewn suits.
The QuikDry hydrophobic lining dries in minutes, so the second dive of the day does not start with a cold, wet suit.
Forum owners rate the warmth a class above the rated thickness — reports of the Aqua Lock keeping pace with thicker conventional suits are common.
The PK G-Lock water-resistant back zipper closes off the usual exchange path along the spine and lumbar.
cons: The sealing system only reaches its semi-dry potential with the matching Aqua Lock hood, gloves and boots, all sold separately.
It is one of the most expensive stock 7 mm wetsuits sold — the 5 mm version already lists around $575 at retail.
Pulling the collar open mid-dive lets a gulp of water run through the suit, so the neck seal discipline matters.
